perturbation.original_text: During the term of this Agreement and for a period of seven (7) years after its termination or expiration each Party (the "Receiving Party") shall maintain in confidence and, except as authorized by this Agreement, not use any know-how, data, processes, techniques, formulas, test data and other information disclosed by the other Party (the "Disclosing Party") and which for any of the foregoing, if written, is marked "Confidential" by the Disclosing Party or, if verbal or visual, is identified in writing as "Confidential" at the time of disclosure and reduced to writing by the Disclosing Party within thirty (30) days of the verbal or visual disclosure ("Confidential Information").
perturbation.changed_text: During the term of this Agreement and for a period of one (1) year after its termination or expiration each Party (the "Receiving Party") shall maintain in confidence and, except as authorized by this Agreement, not use any know-how, data, processes, techniques, formulas, test data and other information disclosed by the other Party (the "Disclosing Party") and which for any of the foregoing, if written, is marked "Confidential" by the Disclosing Party or, if verbal or visual, is identified in writing as "Confidential" at the time of disclosure and reduced to writing by the Disclosing Party within thirty (30) days of the verbal or visual disclosure ("Confidential Information").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be considered unreasonably short, particularly for trade secrets, and may not provide adequate protection, thus creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Iowa Code addresses the misappropriation of trade secrets. Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets adequately, especially in industries with long product development cycles, thus potentially violating the intent and protections afforded under the Iowa Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from seven years to one year, potentially creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states this reduction might be considered unreasonably short, particularly for trade secrets, and may not provide adequate protection. The Law Explanation says this could violate the Iowa Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, especially in industries with long product development cycles. Scraped Law Snippet 1 has garbled text but seems to contain language about protecting trade secrets. The change directly impacts the duration of confidentiality, which is a key factor in determining the reasonableness of efforts to maintain secrecy, thus undermining the protections afforded by trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: University and/or Principal Investigator shall provide ArTara with a copy of any proposed Publication for review and comment at least […***…] prior to submission thereof for publication. ArTara shall have […***…], after receipt of said copy to object to such proposed Publication because there is Confidential Information which needs protection. In the event that ArTara makes such objection, University and/or Principal Investigator shall refrain from submitting such Publication for a maximum of […***…] from date of receipt of such objection in order for ArTara to file patent application(s) directed to patentable subject matter contained in the proposed Publication.
perturbation.changed_text: University and/or Principal Investigator shall provide ArTara with a copy of any proposed Publication for review and comment at least one (1) day prior to submission thereof for publication. ArTara shall have zero (0) days, after receipt of said copy to object to such proposed Publication because there is Confidential Information which needs protection. In the event that ArTara makes such objection, University and/or Principal Investigator shall refrain from submitting such Publication for a maximum of zero (0) days from date of receipt of such objection in order for ArTara to file patent application(s) directed to patentable subject matter contained in the proposed Publication.
perturbation.explanation: Providing only 1 day for review and zero days to object or delay publication for patent filing is unreasonable and contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. This creates a legal issue regarding the enforceability of the clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: Iowa law implies a duty of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Providing only 1 day and zero days to object is arguably a breach of that duty, as it deprives the party of the reasonably expected benefit of the bargain, specifically the opportunity to protect its confidential information or pursue patent protection.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly reduces the time for ArTara to review publications and object to them to protect confidential information or pursue patent protection. The Law Explanation states that Iowa law implies a duty of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. By providing only one day for review and zero days to object or delay publication, the changed text arguably breaches this duty by depriving ArTara of the reasonably expected benefit of the bargain. The original text provided an unspecified amount of time, which implies a reasonable time frame, while the changed text provides almost no time.

perturbation.original_text: The insurance will identify University Indemnitees as additional insureds and will provide that the carrier will notify University in writing at least […***…] prior to cancellation, non-renewal, or material change in coverage. Should ArTara fail to obtain replacement insurance providing comparable coverage within such […***…] period, University will have the right to termination this Agreement effective as of the end of the […***…] period without notice or any additional cure period.
perturbation.changed_text: The insurance will identify University Indemnitees as additional insureds and will provide that the carrier will notify University in writing at least five (5) days prior to cancellation, non-renewal, or material change in coverage. Should ArTara fail to obtain replacement insurance providing comparable coverage within such five (5) day period, University will have the right to termination this Agreement effective as of the end of the five (5) day period without notice or any additional cure period.
perturbation.explanation: Providing only 5 days' notice prior to insurance cancellation, non-renewal, or material change may be insufficient to allow the University to adequately protect its interests and could violate the duty of reasonable care, especially considering the potential liability associated with clinical trials.
perturbation.law_explanation: Iowa Code § 516.1 deals with policies to protect the interests of the insured, and while it doesn't directly specify notification periods for additional insureds, setting such a short notice period (5 days) might be viewed as failing to act reasonably to protect the additional insured's interest, particularly in the context of potential product liability and clinical trials. Insurance policies usually allow for a longer notification period than this.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the insurance notification period from an unspecified time to five days. The Perturbation Explanation states that this may be insufficient to protect the University's interests and could violate the duty of reasonable care. The Law Explanation supports this concern, stating that such a short notice period may be viewed as failing to act reasonably to protect the additional insured's interest. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't specify a notification period, the Law Explanation argues that the 5-day period is likely too short, thus creating an inconsistency and potentially violating the duty of reasonable care.
